coredata.dc:description: Pharmaceutical pollutants such as Amoxicillin, Ciprofloxacin, and Diclofenac pose significant risks to both aquatic ecosystems and human health due to their persistence in water sources. This study investigates a sustainable approach to mitigating these pollutants through the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from Rosa indica petals combined with zinc oxide (ZnO), forming a nanocomposite (CDs/ZnO-H400) via hydrothermal and calcination methods. The nanocomposite's photocatalytic efficiency was evaluated under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation toward the degradation the three pollutants. The CDs/ZnO-H400 showed superior photocatalytic activity, achieving 99 % degradation of Ciprofloxacin in 60 min, 99 % degradation of Amoxicillin in 100 min, and 64 % degradation of Diclofenac in 240 min. The enhanced performance is attributed to improved electron-hole separation, increased light absorption, and excellent stability, allowing for reusability over five cycles.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pharmaceutical pollutants such as Amoxicillin, Ciprofloxacin, and Diclofenac pose significant risks to both aquatic ecosystems and human health due to their persistence in water sources. This study investigates a sustainable approach to mitigating these pollutants through the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from Rosa indica petals combined with zinc oxide (ZnO), forming a nanocomposite (CDs/ZnO-H400) via hydrothermal and calcination methods. The nanocomposite's photocatalytic efficiency was evaluated under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation toward the degradation the three pollutants. The CDs/ZnO-H400 showed superior photocatalytic activity, achieving 99 % degradation of Ciprofloxacin in 60 min, 99 % degradation of Amoxicillin in 100 min, and 64 % degradation of Diclofenac in 240 min. The enhanced performance is attributed to improved electron-hole separation, increased light absorption, and excellent stability, allowing for reusability over five cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of pharmaceuticals like Ciprofloxacin, Amoxicillin, and Diclofenac in the environment has significant negative consequences for ecosystems and human health [1–3]. Ciprofloxacin, a widely used antibiotic, can promote the development of antibiotic-resistant bacteria when it accumulates in water systems. This resistance can spread through aquatic microbial communities, posing a global health risk by reducing the effectiveness of essential antibiotics [4–8]. Amoxicillin, another commonly used antibiotic, contributes similarly to antibiotic resistance and disrupts the balance of microbial ecosystems. Its persistence in aquatic environments can affect nutrient cycles and the health of aquatic organisms [9–11]. Diclofenac, a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), is particularly toxic to aquatic species, especially fish and amphibians. Studies have shown that diclofenac exposure leads to liver and kidney damage in fish, as well as reduced reproductive success. The chronic presence of these drugs in water systems not only harms aquatic life but also bioaccumulates, potentially entering the food chain and impacting higher organisms, including humans [12–16]. Additionally, these pollutants interfere with the ecological balance of water bodies, leading to long-term detrimental effects on biodiversity and ecosystem services.

Photocatalysis is a process wherein a photocatalyst utilizes light energy to accelerate a chemical reaction, commonly decomposing contaminants into beneficial byproducts such as water and carbon dioxide [50–52]. The mechanism entails the absorption of light by the photocatalyst, such as Carbon dots (CDs) or Zinc Oxide (ZnO), which excites electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, resulting in the formation of electron-hole pairs. These pairs engage with water and oxygen to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions, which can decompose organic contaminants and eliminate microbes [53–55]. The beneficial effects of photocatalysis involve its functionality under ambient settings, utilization of abundant solar energy, and the complete mineralization of pollutants without generating secondary waste. Nonetheless, it encounters constraints including inadequate visible light absorption for UV-active photocatalysts, photo corrosion, and charge carrier recombination, which diminish efficiency. Creating enhanced photocatalysts can address these difficulties and enhance performance [56,57].

CDs, nanoscale carbon-based materials with excellent optical properties, have gained increasing attention for their potential in environmental applications, particularly in photocatalytic water treatment [17–19]. Their unique structure, high surface area, and tunable photoluminescence make them highly effective in capturing and utilizing light energy for the degradation of organic pollutants. CDs are particularly attractive because they can be synthesized from various carbon-rich materials, and their environmental impact can be minimized by using renewable and natural sources [20–22]. One such method of sustainable synthesis the CDs is the hydrothermal approach, which employs water-based reactions under high pressure and temperature [23–25]. CDs are crucial in diminishing the bandgap by incorporating intermediate energy levels into the band structure of the photocatalyst. These intermediate states enhance light absorption, broadening the material's sensitivity from the ultraviolet (UV) to the visible light spectrum. Furthermore, CDs can function as electron reservoirs, sequestering photoexcited electrons and inhibiting electron hole recombination, hence improving the total photocatalytic efficiency. This synergistic impact enhances the material's capacity to capture sunlight and decompose contaminants efficiently [58–60].

Zinc oxide (ZnO) is an extensively researched semiconductor with a bandgap of roughly 3.37 eV, rendering it suitable for numerous photocatalytic applications [26,27]. In term of photocatalytic process, ZnO absorbs UV light, generating electron-hole pairs that engage in redox processes, hence promoting the breakdown of organic contaminants, including colors and medicines, in water. Its extensive surface area, chemical stability, and capacity to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) enhance its efficacy in degrading pollutants upon light exposure [28–30]. Moreover, ZnO may be readily changed or doped with other materials, hence improving its photocatalytic efficacy and broadening its utility in environmental remediation, positioning it as a viable alternative for sustainable and eco-friendly technology.

This study explores the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots (CDs)/zinc oxide (ZnO) from Rosaceae and investigates their effectiveness in the photodegradation of three prominent drug pollutants: ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin, and diclofenac. By optimizing synthesis parameters and evaluating the photocatalytic performance of these CDs/ZnO-H400, this research highlights a sustainable and efficient approach for addressing pharmaceutical pollution in wastewater. The findings offer a pathway toward the development of greener technologies for environmental remediation, aligning with the broader goals of sustainable development and pollution reduction (Table 1 ).

Rosaceae was collected from a floral market (16°25'46.8"N 102°50'04.7"E) in Khon Kaen, Thailand, during the summer season (March – June 2024). Zinc Oxide was procured from MiniScience Company LLC, USA, and all chemical reagents utilized in this research are of analytical grade. Tap water was collected from Laboratory Department Integrated Science, Faculty of Science Khon Kaen University (16°28'32.5"N 102°49'26.6"E). and Lake water was collected from Plastic Lake, Khon Kaen University (16°28'23.3"N 102°49'09.9"E). The nanocomposites' crystal structure was examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a SHIMADZU XRD-6000 apparatus, featuring a Cu Ka X-ray source (1.5406 Å). The morphology and particle shape were analyzed using TEM (Hitachi H9500, Tokyo, Japan) and SEM (JSM-5600 JEOL, Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). The sample's elemental compositions and bonding states were assessed using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) using a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source (10 kV, 1500 W, pass energy = 40 eV). The bandgap energy of the samples was calibrated with the Kubelka-Munk equation based on UV–Vis DRS data from the SHIMADZU UV-2600. The PL analysis with Versa-STAT-3. A KH-200 thermal autoclave has been employed, functioning at a pressure of ≤3 MPa. 25 mm Nylon syringe filter 0.22um (Labfil, Zhejiang, China).

The petals of Rosa indica were washed many times with DI water, dried in an oven at 80 °C for 72 h, ground into a powder using a mortum (ar, and stored at room temperature. To synthesize the carbon dots (CDs), about 1 g of Rosa indica petal powder was combined with 50 ml of DI water in a hydrothermal Teflon, which was subsequently placed in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h, after finished synthesis the CDs was centrifuge in 10,000 RPM for 10 min, and filter using filter nylon membrane 0.22um, following Nugroho et al. [5]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis the Carbon Dots/Zinc Oxide by hydrothermal methods (CDs/ZnO-H)
ce:section.ce:para: 25 ml of CDs was combined with 2 g of ZnO and stirred at room temperature using a magnetic stirrer for 12 h. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a hydrothermal Teflon vessel and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h. Upon completion of the synthesis, the product was dried in an oven at 70 °C for 24 h, resulting in the final product CDs/ZnO-H.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis the Carbon Dots/Zinc Oxide by calcination methods (CDs/ZnO-H400)
ce:section.ce:para: After synthesis the CDs/ZnO-H, 1 g of the powder was made and subjected to calcination at 400 °C for 4 h using a closed system and the final product is denoted as CDs/ZnO-H400.

The photocatalytic activity was evaluated using a 125 W mercury lamp to degrade pharmaceutical pollutants. A 50 mg dose of the photocatalyst was mixed with 200 mL of a 10 mg/L pollutant solution, stirred in darkness for 60 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium, then irradiated. Samples were centrifuged, and the supernatant was analyzed using UV–vis spectroscopy at 270 nm for Ciprofloxacin, 410 nm for Amoxicillin, and 282 nm for Diclofenac. Photodegradation efficiency was calculated as, photoactivity (%) = (1 − C/C0) × 100 %) and pollutant degradation followed first-order kinetic was calculated as, ln (C0/C) = k1t. Various factors, including pH, catalyst loading, and pollutant concentration, were studied, and scavengers (IPA, KI, BQ) were used to identify the main reactive species responsible for pollutant degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics and electrochemical test of catalyst powder
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis DRS of the catalyst powders are presented in Fig. 1a. The Kubelka–Munk equation was utilized to understand the band gap energy of each catalyst powder. Fig. 1b depicts this process. The absorption edge value of 387 nm was measured for ZnO. The band gap values for bare CDs, ZnO, CDs/ZnO-H, and CDs/ZnO-H400 were found to be 3.17, 3.04, 2.77, and 2.69 eV, respectively. The CDs/ZnO composite which was calcined at 400 °C demonstrates a reduced bandgap energy relative to CDs, ZnO, and CDs/ZnO-H, attributable to improved crystallinity, intensified contact of carbon dots and ZnO, and the creation of oxygen vacancies. Calcination enhances the crystallinity of ZnO, diminishes imperfections, and facilitates superior integration of CDs into the ZnO lattice, hence narrowing the bandgap. The data demonstrates that CDs/ZnO-H400 synthesized through various ways display a heterojunction structure, as shown by the presence of a singular energy bandgap reflected in the results. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra in Fig. 1c indicate that the CDs/ZnO-H400 photocatalyst exhibits the lowest intensity relative to the other samples. This indicates that CDs/ZnO-H400 exhibits the minimal rate of electron-hole recombination, hence enhancing its efficacy in pollutant degradation or facilitating chemical reactions. The diminished PL intensity underscores the correlation between the material's bandgap energy and its photocatalytic efficacy. A reduced bandgap allows the material to absorb a greater amount of light, particularly visible light, hence improving photocatalysis. The diminished PL intensity indicates a lower recombination of electrons and holes, resulting in a greater availability for photocatalytic activity. Consequently, CDs/ZnO-H400, characterized by its diminished bandgap and reduced photoluminescence intensity, exhibits enhanced efficiency in photocatalysis because to its superior utilization of light energy [31,32]. The crystal catalyst powder was analyzed using XRD. The various catalyst powders CDs/ZnO under different synthesis conditions demonstrate that CDs/ZnO-H and CDs/ZnO-H400 (Fig. 1d) exhibit distinct crystal peaks and show the peaks 2θ of ZnO at31.7° (100), 34.8° (002), 36.4° (101), 47.7° (102), 56.6° (110), 62.9° (103), and 67.8° (112), along with a peak of CDs at 20.4° (002). the peak crystal of CDs didn't show in the peaks of CDs/ZnO-H, and CDs/ZnO-H400 due to the Scherrer effect, crystal carbon dot peaks in the XRD pattern is likely due to their amorphous or nanocrystalline nature, small size, low concentration, or the dominance of the ZnO phase in the mixture [33,34]. The sizes of crystal obtained from XRD data were calculate using the equation
(1)
D =
kλ / β
cos
θ
.

The average crystallite sizes for CDs, CDs/ZnO-H, and CDs/ZnO-H400 are 0.17 nm, 41.5 nm, and 36.8 nm, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to investigate the charge transfer resistance and the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. Fig. 1e shows that the CDs/ZnO-H400 composite displays the smallest semicircle diameter, indicating reduced charge transfer resistance, facilitating the movement of electrons and holes within the material, thereby enhancing charge separation and improving overall photocatalytic efficiency by minimizing energy loss during the reaction. The transient photocurrent response of catalyst powder has been analyzed in the Fig. 1f. The result shows that the highest photocurrent intensity was detected from the CDs/ZnO-H400, compared to those obtained from other samples, indicating enhanced charge transfer and separation, which contributes to improved photocatalytic efficacy [44,45]. The Cyclic voltammetry test of catalyst powder has been analyst, and it's shows the CDs/ZnO-H400 has the higher specific capacitance compare than CDs/ZnO, that mean facilitates efficient charge transfer and reduces recombination, allowing sustained production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for pollutant degradation [61,62].

The Morphology of various powder has been analysist by using HR-TEM and FIB-SEM, as shown in the Fig. 2 . As clearly seen in Fig. 2a, the FIB-SEM image morphology of the CDs/ZnO-H400 composite reveals a mixture of nanoparticles, with a visible large spherical structure that could be an agglomerated particle or a larger ZnO crystal. A high degree of aggregation of the particles, possibly due to sintering at high temperatures during the synthesis. Fig. 2b shows the EDS mapping of the sample illustrating the elemental distribution of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and zinc (Zn) throughout the composite. The existence of three separate colors indicates that C (red) is probably linked to the CDs, which are dispersed within or around the ZnO matrix, whereas O (green) and Zn (blue) delineate the ZnO phase, exhibiting a reasonably uniform distribution throughout the composite. This verifies that carbon dots are uniformly distributed within the ZnO matrix, with ZnO as the predominant constituent. The result from HR-TEM image of CDs (Fig. 2c) reveals uniformly distributed nanoparticles. The image presumably depicts the nanoscale architecture of the carbon dots, signifying their diminutive size and spherical morphology. The distinct, minute dots observable at elevated magnification validate the homogeneity and nanoscale size of the CDs, which are essential for their optical and electrical characteristics. Fig. 2d and Fig. 2e shows the HR-TEM of CDs/ZnO-H400, presented at various magnifications, revealing larger particles with more defined structures, possibly corresponding to ZnO, while the tiny dots or less distinct regions may represent carbon dots adorning the surface of ZnO. The morphology illustrates the dispersion of CDs on or within the ZnO matrix, indicating potential integration between the two components. The quantum yield (QY) of CDs/ZnO-H400 was analyzed using methods from a|e - UV–Vis-IR Spectral Software, revealing a quantum yield of 64 % relative to quinine sulphate dye [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of various pollutant (Ciprofloxacin, Amoxicillin and Diclofenac) by using photocatalytic
ce:section.ce:para: The photodegradation of ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin, and diclofenac was examined in this study. Fig. 3 illustrates the various pollutants with catalyst powder under ultraviolet illumination (135 W, a mercury lamp). The findings are displayed as normalized concentration (C/C₀) over time and pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics (ln C₀/C) to assess catalyst efficiency. Fig. 3a and 4b illustrate the degradation of amoxicillin under photo illumination utilizing several catalyst powders under different conditions, CDs (dark and photo), ZnO (dark and photo), and CDs/ZnO-H400 (dark and photo). In low-light circumstances, degradation is modest, as anticipated, suggesting that photocatalytic activity is essential. Upon illumination, the photocatalytic activity markedly escalates, particularly for the CDs/ZnO-H400 (photo), achieving 99 % degradation after 100 min, with ln C₀/C, k = 0.046 min−1, R2 = 0.97, indicating the most rapid degradation rate of amoxicillin. The efficacy of pure ZnO and CDs is much inferior, underscoring the superior photocatalytic efficiency of the CDs/ZnO-H400 composite. Fig. 3c and 4d illustrate the degradation of ciprofloxacin under UV light, demonstrating that CDs/ZnO-H400 (photo) achieves optimal conditions for pollutant reduction, with a degradation rate of 99 % after 60 min, with ln C₀/C, k = 0.096 min−1, R2 = 0.96, respectively. Conversely, under dark conditions, there is negligible degradation, thereby confirming the necessity of light to activate the photocatalysts. Fig. 3e and 4f illustrate the degradation of Diclofenac under UV light, demonstrating that CDs/ZnO-H400 (photo) achieves optimal conditions for pollutant reduction, with a degradation rate of 64 % after 240 min, with ln C₀/C, k = 0.006 min−1, R2 = 0.99, respectively. Conversely, under dark conditions, there is negligible degradation, thereby confirming the necessity of light to activate the photocatalysts. The enhanced performance of the CDs/ZnO-H400 can be attributed to improved charge separation, higher light absorption, and better utilization of visible light due to the synergistic effect of carbon dots and ZnO. This makes the composite as an efficient catalyst for the degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants under visible light.

The CDs/ZnO nanocomposite exhibited significantly higher photocatalytic degradation activity for both pollutants compared to CDs alone under the same conditions. Calcination at 400 °C improved electron-hole separation efficiency, boosting performance. This process enhances photocatalytic activity, stability, and resistance to harsh conditions, while removing impurities that could reduce catalyst effectiveness. Scheme (2–7) illustrates the potential photocatalytic process for Amox, CIP, and Diclofenac degradation using the CDs/ZnO-H400 nanocomposite, where the heterogeneous connections facilitate efficient charge carrier migration and transfer [48,49].

The optimal conditions for photodegradation have been investigated. Fig. 4 illustrates the impact of different variables on the photocatalytic degradation of amoxicillin, ciprofloxacin, and diclofenac, encompassing the effects of pollutant concentration, catalyst dosage, and pH. Fig. 4a and 5b illustrate the result. Different pollutant concentrations at 5, 10, and 20 ppm exhibit rapid deterioration at lower levels (5 ppm and 10 ppm), with nearly total degradation occurring within 40 min of UV light exposure. At elevated concentrations (20 ppm), the degradation process is protracted, requiring more time to achieve completion. In the case of diclofenac removal, Fig. 4c exhibits a comparable trend, indicating that increased concentrations result in diminished degradation rates. A low concentration of pollutants accelerates photodegradation mostly due to the availability of active sites. At reduced pollutant concentrations, there are fewer pollutant molecules vying for the active sites on the photocatalyst's surface. This facilitates the adsorption and effective breakdown of more pollutant molecules, but at elevated concentrations, the active sites reach saturation, hence impeding the degradation process [4,5,35]. Effect of Catalyst Dosage on Photodegradation, of amoxicillin (Fig. 4d), ciprofloxacin (Fig. 4e), and diclofenac (Fig. 4f) using different amounts of catalyst (25, 50 and 75 mg) has been studied. These three pollutants show the same trend, i.e., increasing the catalyst dosage enhances the degradation rate. The % degradation of amoxicillin and ciprofloxacin show complete degradation within 40 min when 75 mg of catalyst is used. Diclofenac, while following the same trend, shows relatively slower degradation compared to the other pollutants. This indicates that a higher amount of catalyst provides more active sites for the photocatalytic reaction, improving the degradation rate [36,37]. Effect of solution pH (pH = 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) on pollutant removal have also been studied. In the case of amoxicillin, Fig. 4g exhibits the most rapid breakdown at acidic circumstances (pH 3 and pH 5), with the degradation rate diminishing as pH increases. The degradation of amoxicillin by CDs/ZnO-H400 is more efficacious at acidic pH values owing to augmented adsorption, heightened ionization of the amoxicillin molecule, and elevated generation of reactive species. The interplay of these processes, along with diminished recombination rates and lowered scavenging effects, results in the fast degradation of amoxicillin at pH 3 and pH 5, but degradation lessens at elevated pH levels [38,39]. Ciprofloxacin (Fig. 4h) has a comparable trend, with accelerated breakdown at acidic to neutral pH levels (pH 3 and pH 7) and decreased rates at elevated pH. CDs/ZnO-H400 attains maximal degradation of ciprofloxacin at pH values of 7 and 9, attributable to the advantageous ionization state of the drug and the augmented generation of reactive species that efficiently decompose the contaminant. At elevated pH levels exceeding 9, heightened concentrations of hydroxide ions can impede the formation of reactive species, leading to reduced degradation rates [40,41]. The effect of various pH in Diclofenac removal was shown in Fig. 4i. It can be seen that the optimum condition for degradation by using catalyst powder CDs/ZnO-H400 is pH = 5. This is due to the fact that the acidic environment enhances the adsorption of diclofenac onto the positively charged catalyst surface, facilitating more effective interaction with reactive species [42,43]. Varying the concentration of CDs in a CDs/ZnOH-400 composite is essential for optimizing photocatalytic efficiency by balancing light absorption, charge separation, and surface area effects. Fig. 4j, Fig. 4k and Fig. 4l show the effects of various CDs in the photodegradation in amoxicillin, ciprofloxacin and diclofenac. The optimum concentration condition for photodegradation of pollutant is 25 mL of CDs so that 99 % of amoxicillin, 99 % of ciprofloxacin and 64 % diclofenac can be removed. The elevated content of CDs can serve as recombination hotspots for photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby diminishing charge separation efficiency and decelerating the overall degradation process. This disparity among light absorption, charge transfer, and surface contact results in diminished photocatalytic efficacy [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of various pollutant under real waste water
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the quantity of the pollutant in the sample tap water and lake water, the pollutant solution was analyzed by using UV–vis spectroscopic method. The λmax of 270 nm for ciprofloxacin, 410 nm for amoxicillin, and 282 nm for diclofenac has been selected. The accuracy of the approach was evaluated using a recovery study using actual water samples. The samples were enhanced with three concentration levels (5, 10 and 20 ppm) of the various pollutant standard solution. The relative percentage recoveries were calculated using the formula:
(8)
% recovery =
C found
/
C added
× 100 ,
where Cfound denotes the concentration of the analyte following the addition of a known quantity of standard to the actual sample, and Cadded signifies the concentration of the known quantity of standard added to the actual sample. The results of this calculation are displayed in Table 2 , indicating that the percentage recovery of tap water samples (Sample 1 = amoxicillin, Sample 2 = ciprofloxacin, and Sample 3 = diclofenac) after spiking within the range of 0 to 75 ppm is 98.62 %, 103.1 %, and 100.06 %, respectively. For the Lake water (Sample 1 = amoxicillin, Sample 2 = ciprofloxacin, and sample 3 = diclofenac) after spike the concentration of pollutant in the average 97.01 %, 99.5 %, and 100.8 %, respectively. The variation in numerical values observed in the %recovery, accompanied by the occurrence of an amount spike of pollutant sample, may be attributed to the potential influence of another chemical that can impact. Fig. 5. Shows the photodegradation of various pollutant in the tap water and lake water sample with pollutant concentration of 10 ppm. The findings illustrated in Fig. 5 emphasize the effectiveness of photodegradation for a range of pollutants, such as Amox, CIP, and diclofenac in actual wastewater (tap water and lake water), as well as the influence of various radical scavengers. In Fig. 5a, b, and c, a comparison is made regarding the photodegradation of Amox, CIP, and diclofenac in both tap water and lake water environments. The degradation rates remain elevated for both water types across all three pollutants, highlighting the effectiveness of the photocatalytic system in various aqueous settings. Minor variations can be noted, however, there is no substantial difference in degradation between tap water and lake water. The %degradation of Amox, CIP, and diclofenac in the tap water and lake water condition is 98 %, 97 %, and 64 %, respectively. This indicates that the photocatalytic by CDs/ZnO-H400 performance remains largely unaffected by the presence of extra organic and inorganic materials found in lake water. Fig. 5d, e, and f illustrate the impact of various radical scavengers—potassium iodide (KI), isopropanol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ)—on the degradation efficiency of Amox, CIP, and diclofenac, in tap water condition. The findings indicate that after incorporation of KI, which mainly targets holes and surface hydroxyl radicals, there is a noticeable decline in degradation efficiency. However, it still maintains a moderate level, highlighting the significance of holes and surface hydroxyl radicals in the overall process. The presence of IPA, which scavenges hydroxyl radicals, leads to an additional decrease in degradation efficiency, underscoring the important role that hydroxyl radicals play in the photocatalytic degradation process. Ultimately, BQ, known for its ability to scavenge superoxide radicals, leads to the most significant decrease in efficiency for degradation pollutant Amox (98 % to 18 %), CIP (97 % to 16 %), and diclofenac (64 % to 34 %), respectively, thereby underscoring the essential role of superoxide radicals in the breakdown of these pollutants. The results indicate that the photocatalytic system demonstrates a strong capability to degrade pharmaceuticals in actual wastewater (Tap Water), although the presence of certain radical scavengers notably influences the degradation efficiency.

Electrons and holes are photogenerated in the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively, leading to the production of reactive species. The energy levels of the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) for CDs/ZnO-H400 photocatalysts can be determined using Mulliken electronegativity theory:
(9)
E VB
= χ −
E C
+ 0.5 E g
(10)
E CB
=
E VB
– E g

E VB and E CB denote the VB and CB potentials, respectively, while EC represents the conventional hydrogen electrode potential of 4.5 eV. Additionally, χ signifies the electronegativity of the semiconductor, with ZnO exhibiting an electronegativity of 5.79 eV, facilitating charge separation and enhancing redox capability. The E VB and E CB values for CDs/ZnO-H400 are +2.64 eV and -0.05 eV, respectively, facilitating enhanced photo-absorption, effective charge separation, and substantial redox potential. Fig. 6 illustrates the proposed pathway of photodegradation for pollutant elimination utilizing this heterojunction photocatalyst. as per prior research, the conversion products of Ciprofloxacin, Amoxicillin, and Diclofenac were identified using LC–MS/MS analysis of samples collected during the reaction. Hydroxyl radicals were created as oxidants throughout the photocatalysis process. Fig. S1 illustrate that Ciprofloxacin, with a molecular weight of 332 g/mol, underwent degradation, yielding numerous predicted products with estimated weights of 88, 243, 270, and 115 g/mol [63,64]. Fig. S2 illustrates that Amoxicillin underwent degradation, yielding several predicted products with estimated molecular weights of 208, 160, and 189 g/mol [65–67]. Fig. S3 illustrates the degradation outcomes of Diclofenac, accompanied by several forecasts yielding estimated products with molecular weights of 261 and 224 g/mol [68]. The ongoing oxidation of these intermediates is ultimately transformed by photochemically generated reactive oxidizing species into simple byproducts, including water, carbon dioxide, amines, sulfates, and simpler organic compounds. The ultimate byproducts of the photocatalytic degradation of amoxicillin, ciprofloxacin, and diclofenac using Carbon Dots/ZnO typically include CO₂, H₂O, and fundamental inorganic ions, indicating complete mineralization under optimal circumstances. However, if the process is not completely completed, intermediates such as hydroxylated or carboxylated derivatives may emerge, potentially retaining toxicity or antibacterial characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability of catalyst powder CDs/ZnO-H400
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic efficacy of the CDs/ZnO-H400 photocatalyst powder remained continuously elevated after five cycles of utilization. After the initial cycle, the powder was filtered and thoroughly rinsed with DI water multiple times. Subsequently, it was subjected to a drying procedure in the oven for 24 h at 75 °C. Fig. 7a demonstrates that after five cycles, the performance of the catalyst powder CDs/ZnO-H400 remained satisfactory and stable throughout the pollutant degradation process. The examination of chemical bonding via FT-IR has been performed on both fresh and utilized photocatalysts. Fig. 7b demonstrates that the FT-IR spectra of both the utilized and pristine photocatalysts are analogous, signifying a considerable level of structural stability. The crystal structure of the CDs/ZnO-H400 was ascertained following the elimination of contaminants. Fig. 7c demonstrates that the XRD patterns before and after the photodegradation procedure remain almost unchanged, demonstrating exceptional structural stability of the catalyst. The average crystallite sizes of the CDs/ZnO-H400 catalysts were also calculated by using the XRD results, along with the Scherer equation as presented in Table S1. Apparently, there are no significant alterations of the crystallite sizes before and after the photodegradation experiments, confirming high stability of the catalysts. A study was conducted on the morphological structure of the synthesized CDs/ZnO-H400 using EDX-SEM, as depicted in Fig. 7d through Fig. 7i. The results demonstrate the uniform morphology of CDs/ZnO-H400.

CDs/ZnO-H400 nanocomposite was highly effective at breaking down pharmaceutical pollutants using UV light. Amoxicillin also reached 99 % degradation in 100 min, with a rate constant (k) of 0.046 min−1. Ciprofloxacin was degraded by 99 % in 60 min, with a k value of 0.096 min−1. Diclofenac, while slower, achieved 64 % degradation over 240 min with a k value of 0.006 min−1. The remarkable performance of the CDs/ZnO-H400 is due to better charge separation, higher light absorption, and efficient use of visible light, compared to bare CDs and pristine ZnO. Additionally, the material showed excellent stability, maintaining its efficiency over five cycles, making it promising for real-world wastewater treatment. The study also tested the photocatalytic efficiency of the nanocomposite in real water samples, including tap water and lake water. In both cases, the material maintained high degradation rates for Amoxicillin, Ciprofloxacin, and Diclofenac, with only slight differences due to other unknown substances in the water. Amoxicillin, Ciprofloxacin, and Diclofenac were degraded by 98 %, 97 %, and 64 %, respectively. The study also found that superoxide radicals played a key role in the degradation process. Overall, the CDs/ZnO-H400 nanocomposite proved to be very effective in real wastewater, making it a promising option for environmental cleanup.
